summary_one_line: Entity-name token whose first byte is 'l' (matching the 'lt' predefined-entity literal) at the parsed name offset

hypotheses.what_input_feature: An XML entity reference whose parsed name begins with the byte 'l' (0x6c), reaching `case 'l':` in the switch on name[0], ideally spelling the predefined entity name 'lt'. The byte that selects the case is the first character of the entity name string passed to xmlGetPredefinedEntity.
hypotheses.why_winner_satisfies: Winner-resolving cmplog seed e987102bebde5d16 carries well-formed XML scaffolding (`<?xml version="1`, `<!DOCTYPE a SYSTEM`) so a parsed entity name reaches xmlGetPredefinedEntity, and the cmplog/vpc I2S stage can substitute the logged switch/strcmp operand to land an 'l'-prefixed name; the true-side count rises (W hits=60 on line 299) where the loser never takes it.
hypotheses.why_loser_doesnt: Loser-blocking naive/value_profile seeds reach xmlGetPredefinedEntity far more often (L hits=390) but always fall through name[0] to `default:` (line 317 T=390 F=0) — they supply entity-name first bytes that never equal 'l', so the 'l' case (line 299) is T=60 for W but T=0 for L.
hypotheses.mechanism_attribution: cmplog and value_profile_cmplog run I2SRandReplace, which reads the post-execution CmpLogObserver buffer for the switch/xmlStrEqual comparison on the entity name and splices the logged comparand ('l' / 'lt') into the input bytes at the matching offset; naive and value_profile lack I2SRandReplace and must hit the exact name byte by chance, so they stall at default. value_profile's distance-bucket comparison feedback does not help here because a string-equality entity-name match is a discrete switch-case hit rather than a smooth distance signal — confirmed by value_profile losing identically to naive.

evidence_trail.claim: The blocker is `case 'l':` in a switch on name[0] inside xmlGetPredefinedEntity, whose true side is globally blocked.
The blocker line is reached via a switch on the first byte of the entity name.
Taking the 'l' case leads directly to an exact string-equality test against the literal "lt".
The winning cmplog seed contains well-formed XML scaffolding so a parsed entity name can reach xmlGetPredefinedEntity.
Winner takes the 'l' case while loser always falls through to default.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: d=1   L 299  T=60 F=0  T=0 F=390  case 'l':  <-- BLOCKER

evidence_trail.claim: Loser reaches xmlGetPredefinedEntity far more often than the winner yet never takes the 'l' branch, so missing the matching name byte (not reachability) is what blocks it.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: d=1   L 317  T=0 F=60  T=390 F=0  default:

evidence_trail.claim: cmplog adds I2SRandReplace, which substitutes a logged CMP operand into the input at matching offsets.
evidence_trail.exact_quote:
scans the input for byte sequences matching one
operand, and substitutes the other operand at those offsets

evidence_trail.claim: naive lacks I2S and value_profile also lacks I2SRandReplace, so neither can splice the matching entity name byte.
Both decisive pairs differ from their losers by exactly the I2S technique.

mechanism_consistency_check.verification_method: Ran `python3 tools/db_query.py lineage --branch 6381 --role W --fuzzer cmplog --trial 5 --seed e987102bebde5d16` and observed two I2S-floor rows (mutation_op = -) in the ancestor chain at depths 8 (c63d13dce4b1a70c) and 9 (8c7bd7ba6dfb824c); the tool's trailing 'I2S-floor signal' line reports '2 ancestor dash row(s) at depth 8, 9', which under the current build is lower-bound evidence of an I2SRandReplace find in the cmplog ancestry.

falsifiability.would_be_refuted_by: A synthetic harness gating on a single-byte switch followed by an exact strcmp against a literal entity name showing naive resolving the 'l'/'lt' branch as fast as cmplog (no I2S advantage), or value_profile resolving it markedly faster than naive (implying value_profile's distance-bucket feedback, not I2S substitution, is what carries the winner).

weakest_evidence_point: Whether the winner's advantage comes from matching the full 'lt' string (xmlStrEqual) or merely the single 'l' switch byte — the branch instrumented is the switch case, so the deeper xmlStrEqual literal may be incidental rather than the gated comparand.
